preamble: The phonetic transformation of a voiceless alveolar plosive /t/ to a voiceless bilabial plosive /p/ is primarily observed as a regular process of regressive place assimilation in connected speech. This occurs when /t/ is followed by a bilabial consonant (/p/, /b/, or /m/), where the articulation of /t/ shifts forward to the bilabial place of articulation of the subsequent sound, making pronunciation easier. While often a synchronic phonetic phenomenon, such regular phonetic biases can contribute to diachronic sound change over time.
phoneticEffects: Regressive place assimilation, where the alveolar place of articulation of /t/ shifts to a bilabial place of articulation, resulting in /p/

examples.note: The /t/ at the end of 'white' assimilates to /p/ before the bilabial /p/ in 'paper'.
The /t/ at the end of 'night' assimilates to /p/ before the bilabial /b/ in 'before'.
The /t/ at the end of 'bit' assimilates to /p/ before the bilabial /m/ in 'more'.

sources: English Micro-Listening Lesson - Assimilation /t/ to /p/ - YouTube.
Tim's Pronunciation Workshop: Assimilation of /t/ and /p/ - YouTube.
Blevins, J. (2004). Phonetic bias in sound change. In A. C. Yu (Ed.), Origins of Sound Change: Approaches to Phonologization. Oxford: Oxford University Press.
